perturbation.original_text: 3.8. The Courts of the Republic of South Africa have jurisdiction over any dispute resulting from this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6. No warranties, representations or undertakings will be valid or relied on, unless placed in writing and signed by both parties. No changes to this agreement will be valid or hav e force or effect unless placed in writing and signed by both parties. 3.7. No party may directly or indirectly make an of fer employment to, or request an interest from an employee or agent of the other party , for a least 1 (one) year of signing this agreement and for at least 1 (one) year after its termination.
perturbation.explanation: Relocating the jurisdiction clause to an irrelevant section obscures its importance. Jurisdiction clauses should be prominent and easily found, not buried within standard contract terms.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Lack of prominence of jurisdiction clause impacting enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: The placement of the jurisdiction clause in a section concerning warranties undermines its prominence and could be argued to be less enforceable. A clear and conspicuous placement of jurisdiction is considered essential for the clause to be binding. Moving it to this section of general warranties makes it less visible and accessible, potentially violating principles of fairness and due process.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ay seek to garnish a portion of the losing party's wages. If the losing party does not work and has no property, the winning party may be unable to collect on the judgment. Some parties come to court seeking provisional remedies, which are forms of temporary relief available in urgent situations. Temporary restraining orders and injunctions are court orders that direct a party to perform a certain act or refrain from performing a certain act. For example, if a party wants to bring suit to prevent the imminent demolition of what he believes is a historic building, he may petition the court for a Temporary Restraining Order to prevent demolition while the suit is filed. A temporary restraining order will last up to ten days. When the ten days have expired, the litigant may seek either renewal of the temporary restraining order or a preliminary injunction. A preliminary Injunction , if granted, requires a party to perform an act or refrain from performing an act until the end of trial. A permanent injunction is a court order that requires a defendant to perform an act or refrain from performing an act permanently. Civil Justice Reform Act of 1990 Civil cases often are expensive and time-consuming. In August 1990, the U.S. Congress passed the Civil Justice Reform Act to help remedy these problems (28 U.S.C.A. §§ 471–482). The U.S. Senate explained that the Civil Justice Reform Act was "to promote for all citizens, rich or poor, individual or corporation, plaintiff or defendant, the just, speedy and inexpensive resolution of civil disputes in our Nation's federal courts" (S. Rep. No. 101-416, 101 Cong., 2d Sess., at 1 [Aug. 3, 1990]). The act ordered each U.S. district court to implement a Civil Justice Expense and Delay Reduction Plan under the direction of an advisory group comprising "those who must live with the civil justice system on a regular basis" (S. Rep. No. 101-416, at 414 [quoting statement of Senate Judiciary Committee chairman Biden, Cong. Rec. S416 (Jan. 25, 1990)]). The advisory groups in each federal district were appointed by the chief judge of the federal circuit, and they generally consisted of judges, clerks, and law professors. These experts prepared a report on methods for reducing expense and delay in civil litigation. The report was then considered by the federal circuit court judges in forming the Civil Justice Expense and Delay Reduction Plan. One major challenge that faced the advisory groups was how to get courts to best use modern technology. Since passage of the act, many federal circuits have authorized the filing of court documents by facsimile and other electronic means, which may include the use of computers. Federal courts have also acted to improve scheduling. The U.S. District Court for the District of New Hampshire, for example, created four separate categories for scheduling civil cases: administrative, expedited ("rocket docket"), standard, and complex. The determination of a case's category is made at the preliminary pretrial conference. Most cases fall into the standard category, which means a trial will be held within one year of the preliminary pretrial conference. A rocket docket case can be tried within six months of the preliminary pretrial conference, if the parties agree and the trial will last no more than five days. Administrative and complex cases are scheduled with special attention. By identifying the length and complexity of a case at the preliminary pretrial conference, federal circuit courts are able to minimize unnecessary delays. In all jurisdictions, preliminary pretrial conferences have become important in civil litigation. The court, after consulting the parties, schedules and holds this conference within a certain amount of time after the filing of the complaint. At this conference, the court attempts to resolve all the issues that can be resolved outside of trial. These issues include the control and scheduling of discovery, the admissibility of evidence, the possibili
perturbation.justification: The original contract text explicitly states that the Courts of the Republic of South Africa have jurisdiction over any dispute. The changed text buries this critical clause within a section concerning warranties. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this relocation undermines the prominence and enforceability of the jurisdiction clause. The law snippet, though providing limited direct support, generally alludes to the importance of fair and just resolution of disputes, which is undermined by obscuring a jurisdiction clause. This structural flaw, as defined in the instructions, directly contradicts the principles of ensuring clear legal obligations within a contract, making it more difficult for a party to understand their rights. Placing a critical clause in an irrelevant section, per the definition of 'Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction,' creates a situation where it is difficult for parties to locate or interpret this crucial clause.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5. When this agreement comes to an end or written demand has been sent, confidential information must be returned to the other party , within 5 (five) business days of the expiry date or the date that the demand was received. Confidential information held in electronic format mus t be destroyed or deleted to protect its confidentiality .
perturbation.changed_text: 4. SIGNATURES Place Place Month Month Day Day Year Year Signature Signature Signature Signature Signature Signature LAW FOR ALL cares about the legal rights of South Africans and have made it our goal to make the law affordable and accessible to all. This contract template has been designed with you and protection of your rights in mind. Although we have taken every care to ensure that this document is accurate and up to date with the law, it is important to remember that our law is constantly evolving and changing.
perturbation.explanation: Hiding the clause about returning confidential information at the end of the contract affects enforceability, where it is less likely to be noticed and followed, reducing the protection of the disclosing party's confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Requirements for proper notice of contract terms impacting enforceability.
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2008 (Act No. 68 of 2008) (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Consumer Protection Act emphasizes the importance of accessible and understandable contract terms. Moving the clause about returning confidential information to a section related to signatures, after a disclaimer, makes it less prominent and less likely to be read. This undermines the principle that consumers (or parties entering agreements) should have adequate notice and understanding of their obligations regarding confidential information.
perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly defines the obligation to return confidential information within a specific timeframe. The changed text buries this obligation within signature blocks and disclaimers, making it less accessible and likely to be overlooked, which contradicts the principles emphasized by the Consumer Protection Act about accessible and understandable contract terms. The Perturbation Explanation clearly states that the alteration reduces the protection of the disclosing party's confidential information, and the Law Explanation supports this by noting that the placement undermines the principle that parties should have adequate notice and understanding of their obligations. The absence of law snippets doesn't negate the clear contradiction created by the structural flaw.

perturbation.original_text: 3.3. Confidential information may not be disclosed to any third party . Information may only be disclosed to employees and/or agents; or if required by law , and such employees and/or agents must bind themselves to this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.11. The Parties must deliver all legal documents, notices or other communications to the addresses provided and indicated as domicilium citandi et executandi: 3.12. The Parties may send any non-legal documents, notices or other communication via Email, but must attach a “read receipt” to each mail sent, to provide confirmation that t he communication has been received and read.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the clause prohibiting disclosure of confidential information to the section regarding delivery of notices significantly reduces its visibility and emphasis. This is crucial because it impacts the core tenet of a non-disclosure agreement: the protection of sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Requirements for clear and unambiguous contract terms.
perturbation.law_citation: Promotion of Access to Information Act 2 of 2000 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Act promotes transparency and access to information, but also implies the need for clarity in contractual agreements, especially regarding confidentiality. Hiding the clause restricting disclosure within the section concerning notice delivery introduces ambiguity and undermines the unambiguous protection of information that should be expected in an NDA. Placing this in a section regarding delivery makes it less likely to be noticed. This could lead to disputes over the intended scope of the NDA, which is to restrict access and to protect sensitive business information.
perturbation.justification: The original contract clearly dictates that confidential information should not be disclosed to any third party, with specific exceptions. The changed contract moves this critical confidentiality clause into a section discussing the delivery of legal notices. This relocation, as explained, reduces the visibility and emphasis of the clause, potentially undermining the protection of sensitive information. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that hiding the clause within the notice delivery section introduces ambiguity and undermines the unambiguous protection expected in an NDA. This creates a structural flaw that weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality provision. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references Promotion of Access to Information Act but is mostly unreadable, only providing the title but does not provide specific details on why this relocation is a legal issue, beyond the title indicating that access to information is a tenet of the law. Because a legally required disclosure is placed in an irrelevant section, parties will find it difficult to locate and interpret the changed text which is a structural flaw legal contradiction.

perturbation.explanation: Moving the remedies for breach and severability clauses to the purpose section diminishes their importance and enforceability. Remedies and severability are critical components of a contract and should be readily accessible and easily identified.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Requirement for fair and reasonable contract terms.
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2008 (Act No. 68 of 2008) (South Africa)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Consumer Protection Act requires contracts to be fair, reasonable, and just. Moving the remedies and severability clauses to a section describing the purpose of the contract undermines their visibility and accessibility. These clauses are essential for determining the consequences of a breach and ensuring the contract's overall validity. By obscuring these key provisions, the contract's fairness and reasonableness are questionable. Remedies and severability clauses should be easily located within the document.
perturbation.justification: The structural flaw introduced by moving the remedies for breach and severability clauses to the purpose section diminishes their importance and enforceability, creating a contradiction. The Law Explanation highlights that the Consumer Protection Act requires contracts to be fair, reasonable, and just, and that obscuring these key provisions questions the contract's fairness. Since the scraped law snippets are empty, the justification relies on the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation, indicating the placement of these crucial clauses impacts their visibility and enforceability, undermining the contract's overall fairness and reasonableness, as emphasized by the Law Explanation.
